translation: How's it going? / Are you OK?
naturalMeaning: Question and answer in one: 'Ça va ? — Ça va.' Both directions, all day long.

examples.text: Salut ! Ça va ? — Ça va, et toi ?
examples.translation: Hi! How's it going? — Good, and you?

naturalMeaning: The vous-form for strangers, elders, and professional settings. Reply: 'Très bien, merci. Et vous ?'
Literally 'if it pleases you'. Written 'svp' in messages. Also how you hail a waiter: 'S'il vous plaît !'

examples.text: L'addition, s'il vous plaît.
examples.translation: The check, please.

naturalMeaning: The tu-version — using it with friends instead of 'svp' is part of getting register right. Written 'stp'.
Warmer variants: 'merci mille fois', 'un grand merci'.
Literally 'of nothing'. Alternatives: 'je vous en prie' (formal), 'avec plaisir' (warm), 'pas de souci' (casual).
Doubles as 'after you' when holding a door. The tu-form is 'je t'en prie'.
For getting attention (waiter, stranger) or apologizing. 'Pardon' is for squeezing past.

examples.text: Excusez-moi, vous avez l'heure ?
examples.translation: Excuse me, do you have the time?

naturalMeaning: In real speech the 'ne' drops: 'je comprends pas'.

examples.text: Désolé, je ne comprends pas. Vous pouvez répéter ?
examples.translation: Sorry, I don't understand. Can you repeat?

naturalMeaning: Softer than 'Quoi ?'. Even shorter: 'Pardon ?' or 'Comment ?'

examples.text: Vous pouvez parler plus lentement, s'il vous plaît ?
examples.translation: Can you speak more slowly, please?

naturalMeaning: Saying this in French usually earns you patience — and a compliment you should deflect ('non non, un tout petit peu').
Leading with 'Bonjour' + this question in French gets far better results than starting in English.

examples.text: Comment on dit 'receipt' en français ?
examples.translation: How do you say 'receipt' in French?

naturalMeaning: More formal: 'Qu'est-ce que ça veut dire ?'

examples.text: Bonjour, je m'appelle Sarah.
examples.translation: Hello, my name is Sarah.

naturalMeaning: Literally 'enchanted'. A woman says/writes 'enchantée' — same sound.

examples.text: Je viens des États-Unis, de Chicago.
examples.translation: I'm from the United States, from Chicago.

examples.text: J'habite à Paris depuis un an.
examples.translation: I've been living in Paris for a year.

naturalMeaning: With avoir, never être — 'je suis trente ans' is the classic anglophone error.

examples.text: J'ai vingt-huit ans.
examples.translation: I'm twenty-eight.

naturalMeaning: Plural in French. In a café, order something first — toilets are for customers.
At the market you'll also hear 'Ça fait combien ?' for the total.

examples.text: C'est combien, le kilo de tomates ?
examples.translation: How much is a kilo of tomatoes?

naturalMeaning: You must ask — bringing it unprompted is considered rushing you. The air-signature gesture also works.
The polite ordering formula — conditional of vouloir. 'Je vais prendre…' is the other natural option.

examples.text: Je voudrais un croissant et un café, s'il vous plaît.
examples.translation: I'd like a croissant and a coffee, please.

examples.text: Pour moi, ce sera le plat du jour.
examples.translation: I'll have the daily special.

naturalMeaning: The answer to the boulangerie's 'Et avec ceci ?' (Anything else?). 'Ce sera tout, merci.'
You'll be asked this everywhere; answer 'sur place' or 'à emporter'. Note café terrace prices can differ from the counter.
Said before eating, to anyone, always. Reply 'merci, vous aussi' or just 'également'.
Short for 'à votre santé' (to your health). Clink every glass, make eye contact — skipping either is noticed.

examples.text: Allez, santé ! — Tchin-tchin !
examples.translation: Come on, cheers! — Cheers!

naturalMeaning: Waiters take this seriously — expect a real opinion, and taking it is good form.

examples.text: Je suis allergique aux arachides.
examples.translation: I'm allergic to peanuts.

examples.text: Où est la station de métro la plus proche ?
examples.translation: Where is the nearest metro station?

naturalMeaning: The hoped-for answer: 'Non, c'est à cinq minutes à pied.' (No, it's five minutes on foot.)

examples.text: Tournez à gauche après la pharmacie.
examples.translation: Turn left after the pharmacy.

examples.text: La boulangerie est à droite.
examples.translation: The bakery is on the right.

naturalMeaning: Don't confuse 'tout droit' (straight) with 'à droite' (right) — a classic mix-up with real consequences.

examples.text: Continuez tout droit jusqu'au feu.
examples.translation: Keep going straight until the traffic light.

naturalMeaning: A woman says 'je suis perdue' — same pronunciation.

examples.text: Excusez-moi, je suis perdu. Je cherche la gare.
examples.translation: Excuse me, I'm lost. I'm looking for the station.

examples.text: À quelle heure ferme le musée ?
examples.translation: What time does the museum close?

naturalMeaning: Spoken French flips it: 'Il est quelle heure ?' France uses the 24-hour clock: 'quatorze heures' = 2 p.m.
The standard shop goodbye — 'bonjour' opens, 'bonne journée' closes. Reply: 'Merci, vous aussi !'
'Bonsoir' greets, 'bonne soirée' takes leave. Mixing them up is instantly noticed.
Only for actual bedtime — leaving a dinner at 11 p.m. still gets 'bonne soirée'.
The whole family: à demain (tomorrow), à tout à l'heure (later today), à plus (casual 'later'), à lundi (Monday).
Specifically later the same day. Casual clipped version: 'à toute !'
For exams and performances the superstitious version is the ruder-sounding 'merde !' — French theater's 'break a leg'.

examples.text: Félicitations pour ton nouveau travail !
examples.translation: Congrats on your new job!

naturalMeaning: The universal casual 'OK, agreed'. A waiter may say it taking your order; friends say it confirming plans.

examples.text: On se voit à midi ? — Ça marche !
examples.translation: Meet at noon? — Works!

naturalMeaning: 'Être d'accord' = to agree: 'je suis d'accord avec toi'. Casual speech clips it to 'd'acc'.

examples.text: On y va ensemble ? — D'accord.
examples.translation: Shall we go together? — OK.

naturalMeaning: 'Pas de souci' is the even more common sibling — some find it overused, everyone says it anyway.
For real apologies. Small stuff just takes 'pardon' or 'désolé' alone.

examples.text: Je suis vraiment désolée, j'ai complètement oublié.
examples.translation: I'm really sorry, I completely forgot.

naturalMeaning: The gracious reply to an apology. In speech: 'c'est pas grave' — one of the most-heard sentences in France.

examples.text: Je peux m'asseoir ? — Bien sûr !
examples.translation: May I sit? — Of course!

naturalMeaning: Both an enthusiastic yes and a warm 'you're welcome'.
The elegant one-word acceptance: 'Un peu de vin ? — Volontiers.'
Present tense doing future work — said whether you're 30 seconds or 15 minutes away, with the same confidence.
Statement version 'On y va.' = let's go. 'Allez, on y va !' adds impatience.

examples.text: Tout le monde est prêt ? On y va ?
examples.translation: Everyone ready? Shall we go?

naturalMeaning: The imperative sibling of 'on y va' — slightly more energetic, works in any register.
Also the conversational 'hang on…' before a thought: 'Attends, c'est pas possible…' Vous-form: 'Attendez !'
Shouted short form: 'Attention !' (watch out!). Vous-form: 'Faites attention !'
Philosophical shrug in two syllables. Opposite: 'tant mieux' (all the better).

examples.text: C'est complet. — Tant pis, on ira ailleurs.
examples.translation: It's full. — Oh well, we'll go somewhere else.

examples.text: Il fait beau demain. — Tant mieux !
examples.translation: It'll be nice tomorrow. — Even better!

naturalMeaning: 'Génial' is the all-purpose enthusiastic adjective; 'super' and 'top' play the same role.

examples.text: Il ne peut pas venir. — Ah, c'est dommage !
examples.translation: He can't come. — Ah, that's a shame!

naturalMeaning: Literally 'no matter what'. As an exclamation it dismisses something as ridiculous: 'Tu dis n'importe quoi.' (You're talking rubbish.)
Grammar flip that trips everyone: literally 'you are missing to me'. The person missed is the subject.
Casual clipped version: 'Qu'est-ce qu'il y a ?' or just 'Ça va pas ?'
The most load-bearing three letters in French — location, existence, even elapsed time ('il y a deux ans' = two years ago). Spoken fast it becomes 'y a'.

examples.text: Il y a un marché ce matin.
examples.translation: There's a market this morning.

naturalMeaning: Casual version: 'C'est quoi ?' — same meaning, half the syllables.

examples.text: Qu'est-ce que c'est, une 'tradition' ? — C'est une baguette artisanale.
examples.translation: What's a 'tradition'? — It's an artisanal baguette.

naturalMeaning: Spoken: 'je sais pas', compressed to 'chais pas' [ʃɛ.pa]. You'll hear it a hundred times a day.

examples.text: Chais pas, on verra.
examples.translation: Dunno, we'll see.

naturalMeaning: Untranslatable and constant: relief ('ça y est, j'ai fini !'), arrival ('ça y est, on est là'), exasperation ('ça y est, il recommence…').

examples.text: Ça y est, j'ai trouvé un appart !
examples.translation: Finally — I found an apartment!

naturalMeaning: The graceful goodbye to someone mid-project or mid-meal — no English equivalent, widely used.
To kids running, friends pouring too much, drivers going fast — the all-purpose 'easy does it'.
